name: Dojigiri Yasutsuna
description: The Dojigiri Yasutsuna is one of the Five Great Swords of Japan (Tenka Goken), forged by the legendary swordsmith Yasutsuna of the Heian period. Reputed to be the sharpest of the five, it is famed for its ability to slay even the most formidable demons and monsters. Legend says that its creation was inspired by a divine vision, granting the smith the insight needed to forge a blade of unparalleled quality. The Dojigiri is most famously associated with the slaying of Shuten-doji, a powerful oni (demon) who terrorized the region around Mount Ōe. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (Raiko) and his retainers used the sword to behead the demon in his sleep after lulling him into a drunken stupor. The sword's name, 'Doji-cutter,' derives directly from this event. Beyond its incredible sharpness, the Dojigiri is believed to possess a spiritual power, able to ward off evil and bring victory to its wielder. It is considered a national treasure of Japan and is currently housed in the Tokyo National Museum, a testament to its historical and cultural significance. Its legacy continues to inspire awe and reverence, symbolizing the triumph of good over evil and the pinnacle of Japanese swordsmithing.
subtitle: The Demon-Slaying Blade of Yasutsuna
origin: Forged by Yasutsuna during the Heian period, inspired by a divine vision.

keyMyths.title: Slaying of Shuten-doji
keyMyths.description: Minamoto no Yorimitsu used the Dojigiri to slay the demon Shuten-doji on Mount Ōe after incapacitating him with poisoned sake. The sword's name originates from this event, signifying its ability to cut down even powerful demons.

keyMyths.title: Yasutsuna's Inspiration
keyMyths.description: Legend states that Yasutsuna received a divine vision or inspiration that guided him in forging the Dojigiri. This divine intervention is believed to have imbued the sword with its exceptional qualities and power.

sources.author: Kanzan Sato
sources.description: Provides historical and technical information about Japanese swords, including the Dojigiri Yasutsuna.
sources.text: Provides historical and technical information about Japanese swords, including the Dojigiri Yasutsuna.

sources.author: Helen Craig McCullough
sources.work: The Taiheiki: A Chronicle of Medieval Japan
sources.description: Offers context to the historical period in which the sword was created and used.
sources.text: Offers context to the historical period in which the sword was created and used.

worshipPractices.name: Sword Consecration
worshipPractices.description: While not directly worshipped, high-ranking swords like Dojigiri Yasutsuna were treated with reverence. Swordsmiths and samurai would perform purification rituals before and after handling such a blade, believing it possessed spiritual power (tama).

festivals.name: Sword Exhibitions
festivals.timing: Irregular, often coinciding with cultural events.
festivals.description: Rare public exhibitions of the Dojigiri Yasutsuna (when accessible) are treated as significant cultural events. Visitors come to admire the craftsmanship and connect with the sword's legendary history, often accompanied by historical reenactments or sword-making demonstrations.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Dojigiri Yasutsuna continues to inspire modern Japanese culture, appearing in video games, anime, and manga as a symbol of power and historical significance. Its legendary sharpness and association with demon slaying also make it a popular motif in artistic representations of samurai and folklore.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Gram is a powerful sword that can cut through anything, even a powerful dragon, similar to Dojigiri Yasutsuna's ability to slay demons.
Harpe is the sickle sword used by Perseus to behead Medusa, a monstrous Gorgon. Both swords are associated with slaying monstrous beings.
Caladbolg is a powerful sword that could cut down multiple enemies with a single blow, an attribute of great sharpness and destructive power reminiscent of Dojigiri Yasutsuna.

associations.name: Steel
associations.significance: Represents the sword's material and cutting power.

associations.name: Sharpness
associations.significance: Dojigiri Yasutsuna is famed for being the sharpest of the Five Great Swords.

associations.name: Five
associations.significance: It's one of the Tenka Goken, the Five Great Swords of Japan.

associations.name: Crimson
associations.significance: Symbolizes the blood spilled by the sword in its demon-slaying legends.

associations.name: Protection
associations.significance: The sword's power could be seen as a form of protection against evil.

associations.name: Star
associations.significance: Evokes the exceptional quality and legendary status of the sword, making it shine above others.

associations.name: Dragon
associations.significance: Represents the powerful and mythical creatures that Dojigiri Yasutsuna was created to defeat.

companions.name: Onimaru Kunitsuna
companions.description: Another of the Five Great Swords of Japan, it can be considered a 'sibling' or companion blade, representing a shared legacy of craftsmanship and power.

companions.name: Mikazuki Munechika
companions.description: A fellow member of the Tenka Goken, Mikazuki Munechika represents another pinnacle of Japanese swordsmithing and a potential rival or comrade in legend.

companions.name: Yasutsuna
companions.description: The legendary swordsmith who forged Dojigiri. While not a companion in the traditional sense, his spirit and skill are intrinsically linked to the sword's power.

companions.name: Minamoto no Yorimitsu (Raikō)
companions.description: A famous warrior often associated with slaying demons, Raikō is a suitable wielder and user of Dojigiri, embodying the sword's purpose.

extendedContent.title: Creation and Forging by Yasutsuna
extendedContent.content:
The Dojigiri Yasutsuna's creation is shrouded in legend, attributed to the master swordsmith Yasutsuna, who lived during the Heian period (794-1185). Yasutsuna, based in the province of Settsu (present-day Osaka), was renowned for his exceptional skill, and his works were highly sought after by the Imperial Court and powerful samurai clans. The forging of the Dojigiri is often linked to a divine inspiration. Stories suggest that Yasutsuna received a vision, either in a dream or through direct divine intervention, revealing the secrets to creating a blade of unmatched sharpness and spiritual power. This vision guided him in the selection of the finest materials and the application of unique forging techniques, resulting in a sword that transcended mere craftsmanship.

While specific details of the forging process are scarce, it's believed that Yasutsuna employed a combination of traditional Japanese sword-making methods and innovative techniques inspired by the divine revelation. The process likely involved folding the steel multiple times to remove impurities and create a strong, flexible blade. The temper line (hamon) on the Dojigiri is a testament to Yasutsuna's mastery, indicating a differential hardening process that gave the blade its exceptional cutting ability and resilience. The name 'Dojigiri,' meaning 'Oni Cutter,' further emphasizes the intended purpose of the sword: to vanquish evil and protect its wielder. The sword's creation was not merely a technical feat, but also a spiritual undertaking, imbuing the blade with the power to overcome supernatural forces. 

The sword's legacy as one of the Tenka Goken (Five Great Swords Under Heaven) solidified Yasutsuna's reputation as one of Japan's greatest swordsmiths. The Dojigiri became a symbol of the pinnacle of Japanese sword-making, embodying both artistic beauty and deadly functionality. Its creation represents a fusion of human skill and divine guidance, resulting in a weapon of unparalleled power and legendary status.

extendedContent.title: Powers and Properties: The 'Oni Cutter'
extendedContent.content:
The Dojigiri Yasutsuna's primary power lies in its legendary sharpness and ability to slay demons ('oni') and other malevolent creatures. Its name, literally translating to 'Oni Cutter,' is a testament to this inherent property. Unlike ordinary swords, the Dojigiri is believed to possess a spiritual power that amplifies its physical capabilities, making it capable of cleaving through even the toughest armor and supernatural defenses. The stories surrounding the sword often depict it effortlessly slicing through monstrous beings, demonstrating its superiority over other weapons.

Beyond its cutting ability, the Dojigiri is sometimes attributed with protective qualities. It is said to ward off evil spirits and bring good fortune to its wielder. This protective aspect stems from the belief that the sword is imbued with the spiritual power of the kami (spirits or deities), particularly those associated with protection and justice. The sword's association with vanquishing evil forces also contributes to its perceived ability to safeguard its owner from harm. This duality of offensive and defensive capabilities makes the Dojigiri a highly desirable and revered weapon.

Scholarly interpretations suggest that the Dojigiri's power extends beyond its physical attributes. It represents the triumph of good over evil, and the ability of humanity to overcome supernatural threats. The sword serves as a symbol of courage, strength, and the unwavering pursuit of justice. Its legend reinforces the importance of spiritual purity and moral integrity in the face of adversity. The Dojigiri's power is not simply a function of its sharpness, but also a reflection of the values and beliefs it embodies. Its enduring fame speaks to the deep-seated human desire for protection and the belief in the power of good to conquer evil.

extendedContent.title: Notable Wielders and Historical Significance
extendedContent.content:
While specific historical accounts of individuals wielding the Dojigiri Yasutsuna are scarce due to its legendary status, it is often associated with powerful figures who embodied the qualities of strength, courage, and leadership. In legends, it might be wielded by a brave warrior tasked with protecting the innocent from demonic threats. The sword's ownership would have signified immense power and responsibility, reflecting the wielder's commitment to upholding justice and defending against evil. While no singular historical figure is definitively linked to the sword, its presence among the Tenka Goken places it within the context of Japan's most treasured and revered weapons, suggesting its ownership was limited to the highest echelons of society.

The Dojigiri's historical significance stems from its representation of the pinnacle of Japanese sword-making and its embodiment of samurai ideals. As one of the Five Great Swords, it served as a symbol of power, prestige, and the unwavering pursuit of excellence. The sword's existence, whether entirely factual or embellished by legend, played a significant role in shaping the cultural narrative surrounding swordsmanship and the warrior class. It served as an inspiration for swordsmiths and warriors alike, encouraging them to strive for perfection in their craft and to embody the virtues of bravery, loyalty, and honor.

Today, the Dojigiri Yasutsuna is a valuable cultural artifact. Its current location is the Tokyo National Museum, where it serves as a tangible link to Japan's rich history and mythological traditions. Its preservation and display allow future generations to appreciate the artistry and craftsmanship of Yasutsuna and to contemplate the enduring power of the legends surrounding this iconic sword. The Dojigiri's legacy continues to inspire awe and admiration, reminding us of the importance of tradition, skill, and the enduring human fascination with weapons of legendary power.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism and Cultural Context
extendedContent.content:
The Dojigiri Yasutsuna is laden with symbolism deeply rooted in Japanese culture and mythology. Its primary symbolism revolves around the triumph of good over evil. The sword's ability to slay demons represents the power of humanity to overcome supernatural threats and maintain order in the world. This symbolism is particularly relevant in a culture where the boundaries between the physical and spiritual realms are often blurred, and where the presence of evil spirits is a constant concern. The Dojigiri provides a potent symbol of hope and protection in the face of these perceived dangers.

Furthermore, the Dojigiri embodies the samurai ideals of courage, loyalty, and honor. The sword represents the warrior's commitment to upholding justice and defending the weak. Its association with powerful and virtuous figures reinforces the importance of moral integrity and self-sacrifice. The sword serves as a reminder that true strength lies not only in physical prowess but also in unwavering adherence to ethical principles. The Dojigiri's symbolism aligns perfectly with the bushido code, the unwritten code of conduct for samurai, which emphasizes honor, duty, and self-discipline.

In a broader cultural context, the Dojigiri reflects the Japanese reverence for craftsmanship and the pursuit of perfection. The sword's creation by Yasutsuna, a legendary swordsmith, highlights the importance of skill, dedication, and artistic expression. The sword's beauty and functionality are seen as inseparable, reflecting the Japanese aesthetic principle of 'wabi-sabi,' which emphasizes the beauty of imperfection and the appreciation of natural materials. The Dojigiri is not merely a weapon; it is a work of art that embodies the cultural values and artistic sensibilities of Japan.

shortDescription: A legendary katana and one of Japan's Five Great Swords, forged by Yasutsuna. Renowned for its exceptional sharpness and demon-slaying abilities.

wielders.name: Minamoto no Yorimitsu
wielders.context: A Heian period samurai and demon slayer, known for using the Dojigiri to slay the demon Shuten-dōji.

wielders.name: Oda Nobunaga
wielders.context: A powerful daimyō of the Sengoku period, who owned the Dojigiri Yasutsuna as a symbol of his power and authority.

wielders.name: Toyotomi Hideyoshi
wielders.context: A successor of Oda Nobunaga, who also possessed the sword, further cementing its legendary status within the ruling class.
